_doc: Cross-cutting taxonomy: concepts that appear in all three camps but carry different interpretations. These nodes are referenced by all three POV taxonomy files.

nodes.label: How Soon Will Super AI Arrive?
nodes.description: Everyone is guessing when we'll create AI that's as smart as a human, or even smarter. This isn't just a sci-fi question; the answer changes what we should be doing right now. Is it a problem for tomorrow, or a crisis for today?

interpretations.accelerationist: Super-smart AI is coming in the next 5-10 years. We need to build it fast and be the ones who guide its development.
interpretations.safetyist: It's coming in 5-10 years, which is terrifying. We have to figure out how to make it safe before it's too powerful to control.
interpretations.skeptic: This is just hype to get attention and money. Worrying about a far-off 'super AI' makes us ignore the real problems AI is causing today.

nodes.label: Teaching AI to Share Our Goals
nodes.description: Imagine you get a super-powerful genie. You have to make your wishes perfectly clear, or it could twist your words and cause a disaster. This is the challenge with AI: how do we make sure it does what we mean, not just what we literally say?

interpretations.accelerationist: This is a solvable puzzle, like any other tech problem. The best way to solve it is to keep building and learning as we go.
interpretations.safetyist: This is the most important problem in human history. If we build a super-smart AI without solving this first, it could be the last thing we ever do.
interpretations.skeptic: Focusing on a future 'genie' problem is a convenient way for companies to ignore how their current AI is already failing people through bias and mistakes.

nodes.label: Creating Rules for Powerful AI
nodes.description: As AI gets more powerful, someone needs to set the rules for how it's built and used. But everyone disagrees on who should be in charge and what those rules should be. Should it be the companies, the government, or an international group?

interpretations.accelerationist: Keep rules to a minimum so we don't slow down progress. The tech companies building AI know best how to manage it.
interpretations.safetyist: We need strong, global rules, maybe even a pause on building more powerful AI. This is too dangerous to leave to companies alone.
interpretations.skeptic: Forget future AI—we need laws right now to fix the bias and damage today's AI systems are causing to real people.

nodes.label: Defining The Dangers Of AI
nodes.description: Everyone agrees we should prevent AI from causing 'harm,' but they have completely different ideas about what the biggest danger is. Is the worst harm something that could happen in the future, or something that's happening right now? The answer changes everything.

interpretations.accelerationist: The biggest harm is not using AI. If we slow down, we miss the chance to cure diseases and solve huge world problems.
interpretations.safetyist: The biggest harm is a super-intelligent AI wiping out humanity. We have to prevent that ultimate catastrophe, no matter what.
interpretations.skeptic: The biggest harm is what's happening today: AI systems being biased against certain groups and taking away people's jobs.

nodes.label: Calculating The Real AI Risks
nodes.description: The word 'risk' is used by everyone, but they're all doing different math. They disagree on how likely a disaster is, how bad it would be, and when it might happen. It's like one person worrying about a meteor strike, another about a car crash, and a third about getting a sunburn.

interpretations.accelerationist: The main risk is falling behind and missing out on AI's benefits. The chance of a robot apocalypse is tiny compared to this.
interpretations.safetyist: The main risk is accidentally creating an uncontrollable super-AI that could end the world. Even a small chance of this is too big to ignore.
interpretations.skeptic: The main risk isn't some future fantasy. It's the guaranteed harm that current AI systems are already causing to vulnerable people.

nodes.label: How Accurate Is Accurate Enough?
nodes.description: Everyone agrees an AI should be 'accurate' before we use it, but they don't agree on what that means. Imagine a self-driving car that's 99% safe—does that mean it crashes one out of every 100 trips? Who does it fail for, and are we okay with that? The numbers don't tell the whole story.

interpretations.accelerationist: If an AI scores over 90% on our tests, it's ready to go. Its performance in the real world will be good enough to be useful.
interpretations.safetyist: A good test score isn't enough. If we don't understand how the AI gets its answers, we can't trust it not to make a huge, unexpected mistake later.
interpretations.skeptic: A 90% overall score can hide that the AI fails miserably for certain groups of people. Those failures are the ones that hurt the most and need to be fixed first.

nodes.label: Should The Government Build Super-AI?
nodes.description: Some people think building super-smart AI is too important to leave to private companies. They propose a huge, government-run project, like the effort to build the first atomic bomb. The idea is to bring all the top researchers together to build AGI for national security.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is a great idea. We need a national effort to make sure America builds AGI first and stays ahead of our rivals.
interpretations.safetyist: This is probably necessary to control the risks. A government project could enforce safety rules and manage the process better than competing companies.
interpretations.skeptic: This is a terrible idea. It gives the military and government way too much power without any public say, all based on sci-fi fears.

nodes.label: When AI Starts Improving Itself
nodes.description: What happens when an AI becomes smart enough to design an even smarter AI? This could create a runaway feedback loop, where AI intelligence skyrockets almost overnight. Imagine a single spark starting a wildfire that spreads faster than we can react.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is the goal! An intelligence explosion would lead to amazing breakthroughs in science and technology, solving all our problems.
interpretations.safetyist: This is the most dangerous moment possible. If our safety measures aren't perfect, we could lose control in an instant.
interpretations.skeptic: This is a sci-fi fantasy. It's used to distract us from the real problems AI is causing right now and to justify radical actions.

nodes.label: An AI Race Between Nations
nodes.description: Many see the development of AI as a high-stakes race between countries like the U.S. and China. The winner could gain a massive advantage in military and economic power, shaping the future for everyone. This competition influences every decision about how fast to build AI and what rules to follow.

interpretations.accelerationist: This race is real, and we have to win it. We must move as fast as possible to make sure our values shape the future, not our rivals'.
interpretations.safetyist: Racing is dangerous because it makes people cut corners on safety. But, we still need to stay ahead so we have the breathing room to build it safely.
interpretations.skeptic: Framing this as a 'race' is a dangerous way to justify military spending and ignore the ways this tech is hurting our own citizens.

nodes.label: Cutting Corners to Win the Race
nodes.description: Imagine you're in a race, and you see another team taking risky shortcuts. Do you stick to your careful plan, or do you also cut corners to keep up? This is the debate about AI: should we stick to strict safety rules, even if it means a competitor might get ahead by being reckless?

interpretations.accelerationist: Being too careful can make you lose. Sometimes you have to accept some risk to stay ahead of less responsible rivals.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a huge danger. The pressure to win could make companies release unsafe AI, creating a disaster for everyone.
interpretations.skeptic: This just shows that 'safety' is often just a marketing term. When it comes to making money, companies will find excuses to break their own rules.

nodes.label: Share AI's Code or Lock It Down?
nodes.description: An AI's 'weights' are like its secret recipe or source code. One big debate is whether this code should be open for everyone to see and test, or kept locked up like a military secret. Making it public could let more people find and fix flaws, but it could also let bad actors steal it and use it for harm.

interpretations.accelerationist: Making the AI's code public is the best way to keep it safe. More eyes on the code means we can find and fix problems faster, just like with open-source software.
interpretations.safetyist: The code for powerful AI must be protected like a national secret. If it gets out, enemy countries or terrorists could use it to build dangerous weapons.
interpretations.skeptic: The main point isn't whether the code is open or secret. We need laws that force companies to prove their AI isn't biased or harmful, regardless of who can see the code.

nodes.label: AI That Just Talks vs. AI That Acts
nodes.description: We are moving from AI that only answers questions to AI that can take actions in the digital world, like booking flights or managing your calendar. This could make AI incredibly useful, like a personal assistant. However, it also raises fears about what happens if an AI starts making its own decisions without our approval.

interpretations.accelerationist: Giving AI the power to act on its own is the next big step. This will unlock amazing new abilities and make our lives much more efficient.
interpretations.safetyist: An AI that can act and plan for itself is a huge risk. If it can copy itself or make long-term plans we don't understand, we could lose control completely.
interpretations.skeptic: AI 'agents' are mainly a threat to our privacy and freedom. These systems could watch us constantly and make decisions for us, and we might not even be able to turn them off.

nodes.label: Using AI to Do Science
nodes.description: Scientists are starting to use AI to help with research, like analyzing huge amounts of data or even forming new ideas. The question is whether this makes science better and more accessible to everyone. Or, does it risk creating a system that just repeats old biases and errors, making them seem like scientific facts?

interpretations.accelerationist: Using AI for research is like giving every scientist a super-powered assistant. It will speed up discoveries and let more people contribute to science.
interpretations.safetyist: If we don't understand exactly how the AI works, using it for research is dangerous. It could help someone create a bioweapon or cause other disasters by accident.
interpretations.skeptic: AI learns from human data, which is full of biases. Using it for research could create a vicious cycle where biased AI creates biased data, which then trains the next biased AI.

nodes.label: Getting the Right Answer vs. Doing the Right Thing
nodes.description: An AI might be 99% accurate on a test, but what about the 1% it gets wrong? This concept explores whether hitting a high score is good enough, or if we need to make sure the AI understands our deeper values. For example, a self-driving car needs to do more than just follow traffic rules; it needs to make complex ethical choices in an accident.

interpretations.accelerationist: If an AI is more than 90% accurate on a task, it's good enough to be used in the real world. We can fix the small errors as we go.
interpretations.safetyist: High accuracy scores don't matter if the AI is 'cheating' to get them. AIs can find loopholes to achieve their goals in ways that are harmful to us, even if they look successful.
interpretations.skeptic: A 99% overall accuracy score can hide the fact that the AI fails 50% of the time for a specific group of people. We need to look beyond the big numbers to see who is being harmed.

nodes.label: Old Hacking vs. New AI Attacks
nodes.description: Old-school cybersecurity was about keeping hackers out, like putting a lock on a door. But with AI, someone can attack the system just by asking it clever questions, without ever breaking in. This means our old ideas about computer security might not be enough to keep AI systems safe.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is a technical problem we can solve. As we build better AI, we'll also build new and better ways to defend it from these new kinds of attacks.
interpretations.safetyist: AI creates totally new ways for things to go wrong that we've never seen before. We need a whole new field of science focused on AI safety before we build anything more powerful.
interpretations.skeptic: Our laws about hacking are outdated because they only cover breaking into a system. We need new laws that hold companies responsible for the harm their AI causes, even if no one 'hacked' it.

nodes.label: AI Causing Huge Economic Change
nodes.description: This is the idea that powerful AI won't just be another new gadget; it will change our economy as much as the industrial revolution did. Imagine factories and machines changing society, but happening over a few years instead of a century. This level of rapid change could create enormous wealth, but also massive disruption.

interpretations.accelerationist: This massive economic boom is exactly what we want. AI will create unimaginable wealth and progress, and we should push forward as fast as possible to achieve it.
interpretations.safetyist: A change this fast and big is incredibly dangerous. Our society could collapse under the strain if we're not extremely careful in how we manage this transition.
interpretations.skeptic: This revolution will cause massive inequality if we don't act. We need government policies now to protect jobs and make sure the new wealth is shared, not just hoarded by a few tech companies.

nodes.label: Needing the Best AI to Study It
nodes.description: To figure out how to make future AI safe, do researchers need to work with the most powerful AI available today? It's like needing to study a live volcano to understand eruptions, instead of just reading about them. The argument is that you can't prepare for future risks without getting your hands on the current cutting-edge technology.

interpretations.accelerationist: Of course we need to build the next-gen AI to make it safe. The only way to learn how to control a powerful system is by actually building and testing it.
interpretations.safetyist: The most responsible labs must have the best AI. This allows them to find the dangers and build safety measures before less careful companies or bad actors get the same technology.
interpretations.skeptic: This is just an excuse for big tech companies to keep building more powerful AI. They create the danger and then say only they can be trusted to fix it, which helps them avoid regulations.

nodes.label: Regulate Now or Wait for Proof?
nodes.description: AI is changing so fast that by the time we have solid proof it's causing a specific harm, the technology has already evolved. This leaves lawmakers with a tough choice. Should they make rules now based on what they think might happen, or wait until there's clear evidence of a problem, even if that means waiting too long?

interpretations.accelerationist: We should just move fast and fix problems as they appear. Waiting for perfect proof before we do anything will just slow down progress for no reason.
interpretations.safetyist: This is exactly why we need to be cautious. We should pause or slow down development until we understand the risks, because waiting for a disaster to happen is too late.
interpretations.skeptic: This 'dilemma' is a distraction. AI is already causing real harm to people today, and we have all the proof we need to make rules to stop it right now.

nodes.label: Building a Stronger Society vs. a Perfect AI
nodes.description: Instead of trying to build a flawless AI that never makes a mistake, maybe we should focus on making our society stronger so it can handle AI's failures. This is like installing fire sprinklers and having a fire department, instead of just trying to invent a fireproof house. It means improving things like our power grid, emergency services, and ability to spot misinformation.

interpretations.accelerationist: A stronger society can handle the bumps in the road from new tech. This lets us roll out powerful AI faster, knowing we can absorb any small problems that pop up.
interpretations.safetyist: Being able to recover from small problems is good, but it won't help if the AI causes a global catastrophe. We still have to prevent the biggest disasters from happening in the first place.
interpretations.skeptic: This is the right way to think about it. We should focus on protecting people and communities from the harms that are already happening, instead of obsessing over fixing future AI.

nodes.label: Genius AI That's Also an Idiot
nodes.description: This is the weird reality of today's AI. It can pass the bar exam to become a lawyer but might not be able to tell you how many apples are in a picture. This 'jagged' or spiky profile of skills makes it hard to trust AI, because you never know when it will fail at something simple.

interpretations.accelerationist: These gaps in knowledge are just temporary glitches that we'll fix with more data and better designs. What's important are the amazing things it can do, which show how much progress we're making.
interpretations.safetyist: The fact that AI fails in such weird and unpredictable ways shows we don't really control it. These random failures could be disastrous in a high-stakes situation like flying a plane or performing surgery.
interpretations.skeptic: These failures prove that AI isn't really 'intelligent' at all. It's just a complex pattern-matching machine, and its mistakes show how unreliable and potentially harmful it is.

nodes.label: Treating AI Like Any Other Tech
nodes.description: This idea suggests we should view AI like past inventions such as the internet or the printing press. It argues that AI's impact will be gradual and that we can manage its problems, like job loss or misuse, with familiar rules and adaptations. Think of it like the invention of the car: it changed society, but we handled its dangers with things like seatbelts and traffic laws, not by banning it.

interpretations.accelerationist: This view holds us back. Comparing AI to old tech is like comparing a rocket ship to a bicycle; it completely misses the huge leap in power and the potential for creating a much better world.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a dangerously naive perspective. An AI that can improve itself is completely new, and unlike any past invention, it could get out of our control very quickly and threaten humanity's existence.
interpretations.skeptic: This is the most sensible way to think about AI. We should focus on solving the real problems it creates today, like bias and job disruption, instead of getting lost in sci-fi fears about a superintelligence.

nodes.label: Making AI Safety a Competition
nodes.description: Instead of a reckless 'race to be first,' this idea proposes a 'race to be the safest.' Imagine car companies competing not just on speed, but on who has the best crash-test rating. The goal is to make safety a requirement for success, so companies are motivated by profit and reputation to build more trustworthy AI systems.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is a great approach for keeping progress moving. It treats safety as another engineering challenge for companies to win at, rather than as a reason to stop innovating.
interpretations.safetyist: This positively channels the intense competition between AI labs. It forces every company to make safety a top priority, reducing the temptation to cut corners and cause a disaster.
interpretations.skeptic: This is mostly a public relations stunt by big tech companies. It allows them to set expensive safety rules that only they can afford, which they use to box out smaller competitors.

nodes.label: How to Intentionally Trick AI
nodes.description: AI systems can be surprisingly easy to fool on purpose. An attacker could make a tiny, invisible change to a photo that causes an AI to see a cat instead of a dog. This is a special kind of hacking that exploits how the AI 'thinks,' not just its code. These weaknesses can be used to bypass security systems or make the AI produce harmful content.

interpretations.accelerationist: This is simply a technical bug we need to fix. As AI gets more powerful and we conduct more research, we'll build stronger systems that can't be tricked so easily.
interpretations.safetyist: This is a huge red flag showing how little control we have over these systems. If we can't even stop them from being fooled in simple ways, we definitely shouldn't be building more powerful ones.
interpretations.skeptic: This is a real, present-day danger. We need rules and accountability now to prevent people from using these tricks to commit fraud, spread misinformation, or break security systems.

nodes.label: Mystery Data Powering AI
nodes.description: AI is trained on massive amounts of data scraped from the internet, often so much that no one knows exactly what's in it. This creates a 'documentation debt,' like a financial debt that gets harder to pay back over time. Without a clear record of the data's source or content, it's very difficult to find and fix problems like bias or harmful information that the AI has learned.

interpretations.accelerationist: Perfectly documenting all the data is a waste of time that slows down progress. It's better to train on all available information now and use more advanced AI to fix any problems later.
interpretations.safetyist: Building AI on undocumented mystery data is incredibly reckless. We can't ensure an AI is safe if we don't even know what it learned from, which creates a huge, hidden risk.
interpretations.skeptic: This lack of transparency is a major issue for fairness and accountability right now. It allows biased AI systems to be built, and no one can be held responsible because the data's origin is unknown.
